abstract: Video fundamentally intertwines two crucial axes: the dynamic content of a scene and the camera motion through which it is observed. However, existing generation models often entangle these factors, limiting independent control. In this work, we introduce OmniCamera, a unified framework designed to explicitly disentangle and command these two dimensions. This compositional approach enables flexible video generation by allowing arbitrary pairings of camera and content conditions, unlocking unprecedented creative control. To overcome the fundamental challenges of modality conflict and data scarcity inherent in such a system, we present two key innovations. First, we construct OmniCAM, a novel hybrid dataset combining curated real-world videos with synthetic data that provides diverse paired examples for robust multi-task learning. Second, we propose a Dual-level Curriculum Co-Training strategy that mitigates modality interference and synergistically learns from diverse data sources. This strategy operates on two levels: first, it progressively introduces control modalities by difficulties (condition-level), and second, trains for precise control on synthetic data before adapting to real data for photorealism (data-level). As a result, OmniCamera achieves state-of-the-art performance, enabling flexible control for complex camera movements while maintaining superior visual quality.

citations.fullCitation: Wan, T., Wang, A., Ai, B., Wen, B., Mao, C., Xie, C.W., Chen, D., Yu, F., Zhao, H., Yang, J., et al.: Wan: Open and advanced large-scale video generative models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.20314 (2025)
citations.justification: This paper provides the foundational architecture for OmniCamera. The authors explicitly state they build upon the 5B-parameter Wan2.2-TI2V architecture from this work, making it essential for understanding the base model upon which OmniCamera's novel control mechanisms are implemented.
citations.title: Wan: Open and advanced large-scale video generative models

citations.fullCitation: Bai, J., Xia, M., Fu, X., Wang, X., Mu, L., Cao, J., Liu, Z., Hu, H., Bai, X., Wan, P., et al.: Recammaster: Camera-controlled generative rendering from a single video. ICCV (2025)
citations.justification: ReCamMaster is a key state-of-the-art competitor for one of OmniCamera's primary functionalities: trajectory-controlled video-to-video generation. OmniCamera is directly compared against it in quantitative tables and qualitative figures to demonstrate superior performance, highlighting its relevance as a benchmark for precise geometric control.
citations.title: Recammaster: Camera-controlled generative rendering from a single video

citations.fullCitation: Luo, Y., Bai, J., Shi, X., Xia, M., Wang, X., Wan, P., Zhang, D., Gai, K., Xue, T.: Camclonemaster: Enabling reference-based camera control for video generation. arXiv preprint arXiv:2506.03140 (2025)
citations.justification: This paper is a direct competitor for another core feature of OmniCamera: reference-video-controlled generation. OmniCamera's authors cite and compare their results to CamCloneMaster to showcase improvements in motion accuracy and artifact reduction, establishing its importance in the context of reference-based control.
citations.title: Camclonemaster: Enabling reference-based camera control for video generation

citations.fullCitation: He, H., Xu, Y., Guo, Y., Wetzstein, G., Dai, B., Li, H., Yang, C.: Cameractrl: Enabling camera control for text-to-video generation. arXiv preprint arXiv:2404.02101 (2024)
citations.justification: This is a significant prior work that introduced an adapter-based method for injecting trajectory-based camera motion into text-to-video models. It represents an important foundational step towards the explicit camera control that OmniCamera seeks to unify and expand across multiple modalities and tasks.
citations.title: Cameractrl: Enabling camera control for text-to-video generation

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "OmniCamera: A Unified Framework for Multi-task Video Generation with Arbitrary Camera Control."

---

### Research Report: OmniCamera: A Unified Framework for Multi-task Video Generation with Arbitrary Camera Control

**1. Authors and Institution(s)**

The research was conducted by a team of authors primarily affiliated with Sun Yat-sen University and Hunyuan, Tencent. Additional affiliations include CityU and PolyU. The authors are:
*   Yukun Wang (Sun Yat-sen University, Hunyuan, Tencent)
*   Ruihuang Li (Hunyuan, Tencent)
*   Jiale Tao (Hunyuan, Tencent)
*   Shiyuan Yang (Hunyuan, Tencent, CityU)
*   Liyi Chen (Hunyuan, Tencent, PolyU)
*   Zhantao Yang (Hunyuan, Tencent)
*   Handz (Hunyuan, Tencent)
*   Yulan Guo (Sun Yat-sen University)
*   Shuai Shao (Hunyuan, Tencent)
*   Qinglin Lu (Hunyuan, Tencent)

**2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape**

The field of video generation has witnessed rapid advancements, particularly with the emergence of text-to-video (T2V) and image-to-video (I2V) models leveraging architectures such as Diffusion Transformers (DiT) and flow matching. These models have demonstrated capabilities in generating videos from textual descriptions or still images, often aiming to unify different conditioning modalities within a single framework. Examples include Wan 2.2 and Seedance, which integrate text and image conditioning for video synthesis.

A specific sub-area within video generation focuses on camera-controlled video generation, where the objective is to dictate camera movement during video synthesis. Existing approaches in this domain typically incorporate various camera motion control conditions, such as textual descriptions (e.g., Wan-Fun-Camera), 3D trajectories (e.g., CameraCtrl, AC3D, RealCam, FloVD), or reference videos (e.g., CamCloneMaster). Some methods also explore camera-controlled video-to-video (V2V) generation, aiming to synthesize a new video by combining the visual content of a given video with a novel camera motion hint (e.g., ReCamMaster, CamCloneMaster).

However, a recurring limitation in the existing landscape is the entanglement of scene content and camera motion within generation models. This entanglement often restricts independent control, leading to models that typically focus on narrow tasks or support only a single conditioning modality for camera control. These models frequently struggle with the inherent limitations of each modality; for instance, text descriptions can be too coarse for precise control, while 3D trajectories are challenging to acquire. Furthermore, current approaches generally do not support the flexible combination of diverse content sources and camera conditions. This work addresses these limitations by proposing a unified framework that explicitly disentangles and allows arbitrary combinations of content and camera controls, thereby aiming to encompass a broader spectrum of video generation tasks within a single model.

**3. Key Objectives and Motivation**

The fundamental motivation for this research stems from the observation that video intrinsically combines dynamic scene content with continuous camera motion. Existing video generation models often fuse these two elements, hindering independent and flexible control. This constraint limits the ability to simulate realistic cinematographic operations and restricts creative possibilities, which are crucial for professional applications.

The paper identifies several specific challenges in current camera-controlled video generation:
*   **Limited Control Modalities:** Existing methods typically restrict camera control to a single modality (e.g., text, 3D trajectory, or reference video), preventing users from leveraging the complementary strengths of different inputs or achieving complex, nuanced camera movements through combination.
*   **Modality Limitations:** Each control modality has inherent weaknesses; text prompts offer coarse control, explicit 3D trajectories are difficult to specify or acquire, and reference videos might not perfectly capture desired motion nuances for new scenes.
*   **Lack of Unification:** There is an absence of a unified framework capable of seamlessly integrating diverse content sources (text, image, video) with various camera conditions within a single model.
*   **Modality Conflict:** A naive joint training approach that mixes data from different control modalities (e.g., text, reference videos, trajectories) often leads to mutual interference, degrading performance.
*   **Data Scarcity:** A significant challenge is the scarcity of real-world data that simultaneously provides high visual quality and precise camera annotations, which is essential for training models that excel in both photorealism and accurate camera control.

In response to these motivations and challenges, the key objectives of this work are:
*   **Decoupled Control:** To propose a unified framework, OmniCamera, that explicitly disentangles video generation into two independent control dimensions: scene content and camera pose.
*   **Arbitrary Condition Pairing:** To enable flexible video generation by allowing arbitrary pairings of camera conditions (textual descriptions, 3D trajectory matrices, motion reference videos) with content conditions (text prompts, images, source videos). This aims to unlock enhanced creative control and encompass nine distinct generation combinations within a single model.
*   **Overcoming Modality Conflict and Data Scarcity:** To introduce methodological and data-centric innovations that mitigate modality interference and address the lack of high-quality, annotated real-world data. This includes constructing a novel hybrid dataset and devising a specialized training strategy.
*   **Parameter Efficiency:** To develop a system that handles all nine distinct combinations of camera and content conditions using a single set of weights, thereby eliminating the need for specialized models for each task.
*   **Achieving State-of-the-Art Performance:** To demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves superior performance in enabling flexible control for complex camera movements while maintaining high visual quality.

**4. Methodology and Approach**

The OmniCamera framework addresses the challenges of multi-task video generation with arbitrary camera control through several key innovations in dataset design, model architecture, and training strategy.

**Conceptual Decoupling:** The core idea is to conceptually decouple video generation into two independent control dimensions: scene content and camera pose. This allows for flexible composition of various content and camera conditions.

**OmniCAM Dataset Design:** To overcome data scarcity and support multi-modal training, OmniCAM, a hybrid dataset, was constructed:
*   **Synthetic Data (UE5):** Approximately 50 camera motion types (20 basic, 30 complex), each with textual descriptions, were defined. UE5 was used to synthesize 500K independent video clips with accurate pose annotations. This synthetic data is categorized into three subsets:
    *   *Same-Scene, Diverse-Trajectory Data:* Generated by simulating multiple cameras in a fixed scene moving along different trajectories.
    *   *Same-Trajectory, Diverse-Scene Data:* Created by applying identical camera trajectories across different scenes with varied subjects and actions.
    *   *Motion-Content-Target Triplets:* Constructed as sets {z_m, z_d, z_0}, where z_0 (target video) shares camera motion with z_m (motion video) and content with z_d (content video). These triplets provide dense, paired supervision signals.
*   **Real-World Data:** Curated from 600K raw internet videos using a four-step pipeline:
    *   *Trajectory Estimation:* Camera trajectories were extracted using MegaSaM and calibrated using CameraCtrl2.
    *   *Trajectory Filtering:* Noisy trajectories were filtered based on smoothness criteria (r_jump, r_complex thresholds) to retain high-quality, stable motions (resulting in 380K videos).
    *   *Trajectory Classification:* Filtered trajectories were categorized into the 50 predefined camera motion patterns based on similarity (translation error (TransErr) and rotation error (RotErr)).
    *   *Intra-class Matching:* Within each motion class, random pairwise matching of trajectories was performed. Pairs with discrepancies below strict thresholds were deemed valid, yielding 300K same-trajectory, diverse-scene real-video pairs. This process provides real-world data with reliable trajectory annotations and categorical motion descriptions.

**Model Architecture:** OmniCamera builds upon the 5B-parameter Wan2.2-TI2V architecture and its VAE. Key architectural components include:
*   **Decoupled Condition Injection:** To mitigate modality conflicts, a strategy is employed where textual prompts and visual conditions (content and camera-motion latents) are unified as sequence-level representations and interact with the noise latent (z_t) during joint attention. Trajectory parameters are processed via an MLP before integration into DiT blocks.
*   **3D Condition RoPE (Rotary Positional Embeddings):** This innovation addresses spatial-temporal ambiguity arising from concatenating different control modalities. Distinct spatial-temporal base offsets are assigned for each modality (noise latent, content latent, camera-motion latent). Positional codes are then computed on these shifted coordinates, explicitly disentangling identical coordinate structures and facilitating seamless multi-condition integration.

**Dual-level Curriculum Co-Training Strategy:** To manage optimization instability and representation conflicts from training across nine distinct generation combinations, a dual-level curriculum is proposed:
*   **Condition-level Curriculum:** This three-stage curriculum introduces conditioning modalities in order of increasing difficulty and granularity:
    *   *Stage I (Text-conditioning):* Focuses on text-based control (T2V, I2V, V2V), providing semantic guidance with the coarsest granularity. This stage aligns with the base model's capabilities and serves as the foundational learning step.
    *   *Stage II (Reference-video Control):* Incorporates reference-video conditioning, which offers relatively coarse global camera motion transfer through in-context learning. This introduces a higher level of difficulty compared to pure text guidance.
    *   *Stage III (Camera Trajectory Control):* Introduces precise 3D camera trajectory control, demanding the finest granularity and most challenging geometric control. This progressive approach prevents optimization collapse.
*   **Data-level Curriculum:** Applied in Stage II and III, this two-substage curriculum integrates the strengths of diverse data sources:
    *   *Substage 1:* Extensive training on large-scale synthetic UE5 data, which provides perfectly accurate trajectories, to establish precise camera motion accuracy.
    *   *Substage 2:* Brief fine-tuning on a curated set of high-quality real-world videos (with reliable trajectory estimates) to restore photorealism and bridge the domain gap without catastrophic forgetting of learned motion control.

**Multi-task Inference:** A dual-condition classifier-free guidance (CFG) strategy is employed. The model utilizes separate guidance scales for text (w_T) and multimodal camera conditions (w_M), allowing independent adjustment of their influence. Random dropping of conditions during training facilitates this flexible controllability at inference.

**5. Main Findings and Results**

The evaluation of OmniCamera included comprehensive quantitative and qualitative experiments across various tasks and conditions, comparing its performance against state-of-the-art baselines.

**Text-Controlled Camera Motion:**
*   **Quantitative:** OmniCamera demonstrated significant improvements in motion accuracy across T2V, I2V, and V2V tasks. For T2V, it achieved 92.4% MotionAcc compared to 31.4% for Wan2.2-TI2V. For I2V, it scored 91.3% MotionAcc versus 68.8% for Wan2.2-Fun-Camera. It also maintained competitive visual fidelity metrics (FVD, CLIP-F, CLIP-T).
*   **Qualitative:** The model was capable of generating complex 3D spatial movements based on textual commands, outperforming baseline models which struggled with specific commands or exhibited content hallucination and structural artifacts (e.g., erroneous features in animals or static volcanic smoke in Wan2.2-Fun-Camera). OmniCamera additionally introduced text-guided camera motion editing for existing videos (V2V), a capability not commonly explored by prior models.

**Reference-Video-Controlled Camera Motion:**
*   **Quantitative:** OmniCamera achieved accurate camera motion control in T2V, I2V, and V2V tasks. It demonstrated superior performance compared to CamCloneMaster, exhibiting lower rotation errors (e.g., 0.019 vs 0.021 RotErr for I2V) and improved content preservation.
*   **Qualitative:** Generated videos showcased accurate camera motion mirroring the reference, with superior content preservation and fewer visual artifacts compared to CamCloneMaster, which produced incorrect camera motions and distortions. This performance was attributed to the dual-level curriculum and hybrid data composition.

**Trajectory-Controlled Camera Motion:**
*   **Quantitative:** OmniCamera achieved highly accurate 3D trajectory control across T2V, I2V, and V2V tasks. It exhibited lower translation and rotation errors compared to baselines like ReCamMaster (e.g., for T2V, 2.064 TransErr vs 7.542 for AC3D, and 0.034 RotErr vs 0.056 for AC3D).
*   **Qualitative:** The model closely followed diverse and complex trajectories, including triangular and polyline paths, with fewer visual artifacts compared to ReCamMaster, which generated distortions on objects.

**Combining Control Modalities and Conflict Analysis:**
*   OmniCamera demonstrated the ability to seamlessly integrate distinct camera conditions (e.g., semantic text command with a 3D trajectory) to produce composite camera effects.
*   An analysis of modality conflicts, where contradictory motions were provided in pairwise combinations of trajectory, reference video, and text conditions, revealed a consistent dominance hierarchy: Trajectory > Reference Video > Text. This indicates that conditions with more explicit geometric information tend to override semantic or less precise inputs.

**Ablation Studies:**
*   **Dual-level Curriculum:** The ablation studies confirmed the necessity of both the condition-level curriculum (CC) and data-level curriculum (DC).
    *   Removing CC led to significant visual degradation (frame deterioration, content hallucination) and representation conflicts, failing to disentangle camera and content conditions effectively.
    *   Removing DC hindered precise pose control (e.g., missing specific camera movements) and resulted in degraded visual quality.
*   **Data Composition (OmniCAM's Hybrid Sources):**
    *   Training without UE5 synthetic data resulted in a noticeable drop in camera controllability, indicating the importance of synthetic data for motion precision.
    *   Training without real-world data led to a significant decrease in visual realism and introduced erroneous artifacts (e.g., grids), highlighting the role of real data in bridging the domain gap and achieving photorealism. The combined use of both data sources was crucial for high-fidelity appearances while preserving learned camera control.
*   **3D Condition RoPE:** This component was found to be essential for disentangling the spatial-temporal structure of multiple control signals. Its removal led to consistent degradation in both geometric errors and visual quality, with outputs showing artifacts and structural inconsistencies due to interference between positional encoding and injected conditions.
*   **Dual-Condition CFG:** This guidance strategy played a critical role in enforcing precise adherence to camera instructions during inference, primarily impacting camera controllability (higher trajectory errors) if removed, with a relatively milder impact on visual quality.
*   **Content/Camera Injection Strategies:** The proposed token concatenation for video-based conditions and sparse injection for camera-control conditions were shown to be the most effective, avoiding issues such as shallow latent information or unstable optimization observed with alternative strategies.

**6. Significance and Potential Impact**

The OmniCamera framework represents a notable advancement in multi-task video generation with camera control, with several areas of significance and potential impact:

*   **Unified and Flexible Control:** OmniCamera is presented as the first unified video generation model that seamlessly integrates three camera conditions (text, trajectory, reference video) with three content conditions (text, image, video). This comprehensive integration, enabling arbitrary combinations within a single model, significantly enhances the flexibility and scope of video generation. It moves beyond single-modality or single-task approaches, allowing for more complex and nuanced creative control.
*   **Decoupling of Content and Camera:** The explicit disentanglement of scene content and camera motion is a core conceptual contribution. This decoupling provides a more granular and independent command over these two fundamental aspects of video, which can lead to greater precision and versatility in generative outcomes.
*   **Addressing Data and Training Challenges:** The introduction of the OmniCAM hybrid dataset, combining high-precision synthetic trajectories with diverse real-world videos, directly addresses the pervasive challenges of data scarcity and the trade-off between geometric accuracy and photorealism. The Dual-level Curriculum Co-Training strategy further enhances robustness by progressively aligning multimodal conditions and effectively bridging the domain gap between synthetic and real data, ensuring stable and effective multimodal learning.
*   **Improved Performance and Efficiency:** The framework demonstrates improved controllability and competitive visual quality across a wide range of tasks, outperforming models trained under single-task or single-condition settings. The parameter efficiency of using a single set of weights for all nine distinct combinations is also a practical advantage, reducing computational overhead and model complexity.
*   **Foundation for Advanced Applications:** By offering fine-grained control over camera movements, OmniCamera has potential implications for professional applications requiring precise cinematographic operations, such as film pre-visualization, content creation for virtual reality and gaming, and automated video editing. It enables creators to iterate on complex camera paths and content scenarios more effectively.
*   **Future Research Directions:** The work opens avenues for future research into even finer-grained controls, such as incorporating multiple reference images or localized motion guidance, which are currently outside the scope of the framework. This suggests a pathway toward increasingly sophisticated video generation systems that offer even more granular command over scene dynamics and observational perspectives.
overview:
## Introduction to OmniCamera

The evolution of video generation has transitioned from simple text-to-video synthesis to highly controlled cinematic creation. While modern models can generate high-fidelity visuals from text or images, they often struggle to separate the movement of the camera from the motion of the subjects within the scene. In natural video, dynamic scene content and continuous camera motion are intrinsically linked, yet for professional content creation, the ability to control these two elements independently is essential.

OmniCamera is a unified framework designed to address this challenge by explicitly disentangling video generation into two independent control dimensions: scene content and camera pose. Unlike prior models that typically support only a single type of camera control (such as just text or just 3D trajectories), OmniCamera allows for arbitrary combinations of content sources (text, image, or video) and camera conditions (textual descriptions, 3D trajectory matrices, or motion reference videos). This unified approach enables nine distinct generation tasks within a single model architecture, providing a level of creative flexibility previously unavailable in specialized, task-specific models.

![OmniCamera Framework Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06010v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: The OmniCamera framework supports nine distinct combinations of content and camera conditions, allowing for flexible multi-task video generation with a single set of model weights.*

## Decoupling Scene Content and Camera Motion

The core philosophy of OmniCamera is "conceptual decoupling." In many existing Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures, camera motion is often treated as part of the overall scene dynamics. This entanglement leads to "modality conflict," where a model might confuse a command for a camera "pan right" with an instruction for an object in the scene to move to the right.

To achieve true decoupling, the framework treats the target video generation as a function of separate content and camera latents. Specifically, the model aims to predict a target video $z_0$ by conditioning on a content source (which could be a prompt $T$ or an image/video $z_c$) and a camera motion hint (which could be a trajectory $M$, a motion reference video $z_m$, or a text description). By defining these as independent inputs, the framework allows a user to, for example, take the visual content of a still image and apply the exact 3D camera trajectory extracted from a different, unrelated action movie.

## The OmniCAM Hybrid Dataset

A significant barrier to training precise camera-control models is the scarcity of high-quality, real-world data with accurate camera annotations. Real-world videos often lack precise 3D metadata, while synthetic data, though perfectly annotated, can suffer from a "domain gap" that makes generated videos look artificial or "video-game-like."

The authors address this by constructing OmniCAM, a hybrid dataset that combines the precision of synthetic environments with the visual richness of real-world footage.

### Synthetic Data Generation
Using Unreal Engine 5 (UE5), the researchers defined approximately 50 distinct camera motion patterns, ranging from basic moves like "Dolly In" to complex polyline trajectories. They synthesized 500,000 video clips, creating specialized data structures:
- **Same-Scene, Diverse-Trajectory:** Multiple cameras moving through the same static scene to teach the model how different paths affect the same visual content.
- **Same-Trajectory, Diverse-Scene:** The same camera path applied to various environments to help the model generalize motion across different subjects.
- **Motion-Content-Target Triplets:** Sets denoted as $\{z_m, z_d, z_0\}$, where the target $z_0$ shares its camera motion with $z_m$ and its visual content with $z_d$.

### Real-World Data Processing
To maintain photorealism, the team curated 600,000 raw internet videos. They developed a rigorous four-step pipeline to extract usable camera information:
1. **Trajectory Estimation:** Using advanced tools like MegaSaM to extract 3D paths.
2. **Trajectory Filtering:** Removing unstable or "jumpy" motions based on smoothness thresholds $r_{jump}$ and $r_{complex}$.
3. **Trajectory Classification:** Mapping these real paths to the 50 predefined motion categories.
4. **Intra-class Matching:** Finding pairs of real videos that happen to share similar camera movements, effectively creating real-world versions of the "same-trajectory, diverse-scene" data.

![OmniCAM Dataset Construction](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06010v1/x4.png)
*Figure 2: The construction process of the OmniCAM dataset, featuring both synthetic triplets from UE5 and curated, annotated real-world video pairs.*

## Architecture: 3D Condition RoPE and Decoupled Injection

OmniCamera is built upon a 5-billion parameter Diffusion Transformer. To handle the diverse and sometimes conflicting input modalities, the researchers introduced two primary architectural innovations: Decoupled Condition Injection and 3D Condition RoPE.

### Decoupled Condition Injection
When providing multiple conditions (like an image for content and a video for motion), a naive approach would be to simply stack them. However, this often leads to optimization instability. OmniCamera unifies textual prompts and visual conditions as sequence-level representations that interact with the noise latent $z_t$ through joint attention. For precise geometric inputs like 3D trajectory matrices, the model uses a specialized Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) to process the coordinates before they are integrated into the DiT blocks.

### 3D Condition RoPE (Rotary Positional Embeddings)
A major challenge in multi-modal generation is "spatial-temporal ambiguity." If the model receives both a content video and a camera-motion video, how does it know which frame of the content video corresponds to which frame of the motion video? 

The 3D Condition RoPE solves this by assigning distinct spatial-temporal base offsets to each modality. This explicitly separates the coordinate structures of the noise latent, the content latent, and the camera-motion latent. By shifting the positional codes for each input, the model can effectively "read" multiple control signals simultaneously without them interfering with each other's spatial-temporal structure.

![OmniCamera Architecture](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06010v1/x3.png)
*Figure 3: The model architecture showing how different conditions are tokenized and processed through 3D Condition RoPE before entering the Unified Diffusion Transformer.*

## Dual-Level Curriculum Training Strategy

Training a single model to handle nine different tasks is notoriously difficult. If all tasks are introduced at once, the model may fail to converge or may prioritize one modality (like text) while ignoring others (like trajectories). OmniCamera employs a "Dual-Level Curriculum" to manage this complexity.

### Condition-Level Curriculum
The model is trained in three stages of increasing difficulty:
1. **Stage I (Text-conditioning):** Focuses on basic semantic guidance (e.g., "camera zooms in"). This aligns the model with the general concepts of motion.
2. **Stage II (Reference-video Control):** Introduces "in-context learning," where the model learns to transfer motion from a reference video. This is more difficult as it requires global motion understanding.
3. **Stage III (Camera Trajectory Control):** Introduces precise 3D coordinates. This is the finest level of granularity and requires the model to have a deep geometric understanding of the scene.

### Data-Level Curriculum
Within Stages II and III, the model follows a two-substage data path:
- **Substage 1:** Heavy training on synthetic UE5 data to establish precise camera motion accuracy.
- **Substage 2:** Brief fine-tuning on high-quality real-world videos to restore photorealism and ensure the model doesn't lose its ability to generate realistic textures and lighting.

![Curriculum Training Strategy](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06010v1/x5.png)
*Figure 4: The three-stage training process that progressively introduces more complex camera control modalities and transitions from synthetic to real-world data.*

## Multi-Task Performance and Control Modalities

OmniCamera demonstrates strong performance across text, reference video, and trajectory controls. Quantitative evaluations using Motion Accuracy (MotionAcc) and geometric error metrics like $\text{TransErr}$ (translation error) and $\text{RotErr}$ (rotation error) show that OmniCamera often outperforms specialized models.

### Text and Reference Control
In text-to-video tasks, OmniCamera achieved a MotionAcc of 92.4%, a significant increase over baseline models. When using a reference video for motion, it successfully preserves the content of the source while mirroring the camera movement of the reference, avoiding the visual distortions (such as "melting" objects or background artifacts) often seen in competing methods.

### 3D Trajectory Control
The model allows for extremely precise control using polylines or complex 3D paths. For instance, in trajectory-controlled T2V, it recorded a $\text{TransErr}$ of 2.064, compared to much higher errors in previous state-of-the-art models. This precision is particularly evident when the camera follows non-linear paths, such as "triangular" or "circular" orbits.

### Modality Dominance
A unique finding of the research is the "dominance hierarchy" when multiple conditions are provided. If a user provides a text prompt saying "zoom in" but a 3D trajectory that "pans left," the model must choose which to follow. The study found a consistent hierarchy:
$$ \text{Trajectory} > \text{Reference Video} > \text{Text} $$
This suggests that the model correctly prioritizes explicit geometric information over coarser semantic descriptions.

![Qualitative Results Comparison](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06010v1/x10.png)
*Figure 5: A comparison of OmniCamera against other leading methods across text, trajectory, and reference video controls. OmniCamera shows higher structural stability and motion accuracy.*

## Significance and Future Applications

OmniCamera is the first framework to successfully unify nine distinct video generation tasks involving arbitrary combinations of content and camera control into a single, parameter-efficient model. By decoupling motion from content and introducing a robust training curriculum, it bridges the gap between high-level creative intent and low-level geometric precision.

The practical implications of this work are significant for industries like film pre-visualization, where directors can prototype complex shots using simple reference videos or hand-drawn trajectories. It also opens new doors for "video editing" by allowing users to change the camera angle of an existing video clip while keeping the action and characters intact. While future work may explore even more granular controls—such as localized subject motion alongside camera paths—OmniCamera provides a foundational step toward truly professional-grade, controllable video synthesis.

keyInsights: Explicitly decoupling scene content and camera motion is crucial for achieving flexible, precise, and multi-modal control in video generation.

A hybrid training dataset, leveraging the precision of synthetic data and the realism of curated real-world data, is essential for robust models that excel in both geometric accuracy and visual quality.
A progressive, dual-level curriculum strategy is effective for stable training of multi-task, multi-modal video generation models, preventing optimization collapse and representation conflicts.
Conditions with more explicit geometric information (e.g., 3D trajectories) tend to override less precise inputs (e.g., text) when multiple, potentially conflicting, camera conditions are provided.
originalProblem: Existing video generation models often entangle scene content and camera motion, hindering independent and flexible control and limiting creative possibilities.

Current camera control methods are typically restricted to a single modality, each with inherent limitations (e.g., text is too coarse, 3D trajectories are hard to acquire), and generally lack unification.
A significant challenge is the scarcity of high-quality real-world data with precise camera annotations, essential for training models that balance photorealism and accurate control.
results: Achieved significantly improved motion accuracy for text-controlled camera motion (e.g., 92.4% MotionAcc for T2V vs. 31.4% for baseline) while maintaining competitive visual fidelity.

Demonstrated highly accurate camera motion control for reference-video and 3D trajectory inputs, outperforming baselines with lower translation and rotation errors (e.g., 2.064 TransErr for T2V vs. 7.542 for AC3D).
Successfully combined distinct camera conditions for composite effects and maintained high visual quality across all nine multi-task generation combinations using a single parameter-efficient model.
solution: Proposes OmniCamera, a unified framework that explicitly decouples video generation into independent scene content and camera pose control, supporting nine distinct task combinations within a single model.
Constructs OmniCAM, a hybrid dataset combining 500K synthetic UE5 videos with precise camera pose annotations and 300K curated real-world videos with estimated trajectories.
Introduces a Dual-level Curriculum Co-Training strategy, progressively introducing conditioning modalities and integrating synthetic and real data to mitigate modality conflicts and domain gaps.
Employs 3D Condition Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) to address spatial-temporal ambiguity and disentangle different control modalities effectively.
summary.summary: OmniCamera is a unified framework for multi-task video generation that disentangles scene content from camera pose. It enables arbitrary combinations of three camera conditions (text, 3D trajectory, motion reference video) and three content conditions (text, image, source video), achieving enhanced control and photorealistic output across nine distinct generation tasks using a single model.

title: OmniCamera: A Unified Framework for Multi-task Video Generation with Arbitrary Camera Control
